title: Saint Erkenwald
summary: The repository XML is a version of University of Michigan CME item CME90053, whose 2023 header says that ME Compendium marked up Oxford Text Archive item 0053 and checked it against Israel Gollancz's 1922 edition. OTA 0053 states directly that it was transcribed from Gollancz. Gollancz based his edition on a fresh transcript of the sole surviving witness, British Library Harley MS 2250, fols. 72v-75v, while consulting Carl Horstmann's 1881 predecessor edition. Complete public scans of both printed editions and the complete DIMEV v1.0 release are cached locally. The exact BL catalogue links an unavailable legacy image object, but its published extent cannot currently be verified; complete-codex photography can instead be requested from Imaging Services subject to condition, price, and reuse permission.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for CME90053
entities.description: The XML artifact consumed by this repository's conversion and publication workflow and supplied through the pinned Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
notes: The file contains IDNO CME90053, IDG/@ID CME90053, BIBNO OTA0053 and VID CME90053.
The approved source path is explicitly bound to CME90053 in the repository publication-set override data.

entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse item CME90053
entities.description: A 2023 ME Compendium structural encoding of the OTA 0053 text, with folio references, line numbers and manuscript readings added by comparison with Gollancz's print edition.

date_statements.label: item/header creation
date_statements.notes: The header says it was created 21 February 2023 and records a 13 February 2023 markup revision.

notes: This is a digital encoding layer, not the abstract medieval poem, Gollancz's edition or the manuscript witness.
The stable item resolver was retained even though the automated item check encountered an access challenge.

entities.label: Oxford Text Archive item 0053: St Erkenwald
entities.description: A legacy plain-text transcription issued by the Oxford Text Archive in 1976 and explicitly transcribed from Gollancz's 1922 edition.
notes: The depositor and original transcription date are not identified; 1976 is the current issue/publication date.
The OTA header calls the resource legacy plain text and documents its normalization and omission practices.

entities.label: Gollancz 1922 edition of Saint Erkenwald

bibliographic.citation: Israel Gollancz, ed., St. Erkenwald (Bishop of London 675-693): An Alliterative Poem, Written about 1386, Narrating a Miracle Wrought by the Bishop in St. Paul's Cathedral, Select Early English Poems 4 (London: Humphrey Milford, Oxford University Press, 1922).
bibliographic.title: St. Erkenwald (Bishop of London 675-693): An Alliterative Poem, Written about 1386, Narrating a Miracle Wrought by the Bishop in St. Paul's Cathedral

notes: Gollancz says the edition is based on a fresh transcript of Harley MS 2250 and records deviations from the manuscript in textual and general notes.
Its quatrain arrangement is editorial; square brackets and obeli indicate emendation, and contraction expansions reflect Gollancz's judgement.
The title's date 'about 1386' is Gollancz's conjecture, not an internally dated fact.

entities.label: Turville-Petre 2024 critical edition of Saint Erkenwald

bibliographic.citation: Thorlac Turville-Petre, ed., St Erkenwald: A Critical Edition, Exeter Medieval Texts and Studies (Liverpool: Liverpool University Press, 2024).
bibliographic.title: St Erkenwald: A Critical Edition

notes: DIMEV v1.0 cites this edition as Turville-Petre2024. The Medieval Review describes a facing-page Middle English text and modern English prose translation with introduction, annotations, manuscript gloss appendix, glossary, and index.
The complete edition was not publicly downloadable or locally cached. It is recorded as collateral current scholarship and is not inserted into the verified OTA-to-Gollancz primary transmission path.

entities.label: Horstmann 1881 edition of Saint Erkenwald

bibliographic.citation: Carl Horstmann, Altenglische Legenden, Neue Folge (Heilbronn: Gebr. Henninger, 1881), 265-274.
bibliographic.title: Altenglische Legenden, Neue Folge

notes: DIMEV cites the textual edition at pages 266-274; printed page 265 supplies Horstmann's heading and German summary.
This is a predecessor that Gollancz consulted, not the immediate source of OTA 0053.

entities.label: British Library Harley MS 2250
entities.description: The sole surviving manuscript witness of Saint Erkenwald, at fols. 72v-75v within a paper religious miscellany.

date_statements.label: catalog date
date_statements.value: second half of the fifteenth century
date_statements.notes: John Watton dated the Speculum Christiani on f. 64v to 1477, and the BL catalog says fols. 65-83 are probably in the same hand; this connection does not make every text's copying date independently certain.

holding.notes: Saint Erkenwald occupies fols. 72v-75v in the current BL catalog and DIMEV.

physical_description.extent: 108 folios plus inserted and unfoliated leaves
notes: Gothic cursive script with small red initials.
The current record localizes fols. 1r-65v to Cheshire and says fols. 65-83 are probably in the same hand as John Watton's dated 1477 section.
DIMEV/eLALME assigns Saint Erkenwald at fols. 72v-75v to Linguistic Profile 419, Cheshire.

The legacy a/b notation is the same leaf-side convention as modern r/v notation: 72b is 72v, and the CME milestones run through 75b/75v. Gollancz's preface and OTA repeat an endpoint of 75a, but Gollancz's poem itself marks [F. 75b] before line 341; current BL and DIMEV records likewise end the work at 75v. The 75a endpoint is therefore preserved as a source-layer error, not treated as evidence of refoliation.
The manuscript title recorded by DIMEV is De Sancto Erkenwaldo Episcopo.
The following Life of St John the Baptist begins on f. 75v, so Saint Erkenwald's final leaf is a shared manuscript boundary.

If access returns, inventory the full published object before assigning coverage. Associate Saint Erkenwald with fols. 72v-75v, noting that f. 75v is shared with the following Life of St John the Baptist.

entities.label: Digital Index of Middle English Verse record 706
notes: Record 706 describes one witness, Harley MS 2250, fols. 72v-75v, and identifies the poem as alliterative unrhymed verse.
The v1.0 data also identifies the work's eLALME profile and supplies a bibliography of editions through Turville-Petre 2024.

agents.name: Digital Index of Middle English Verse

scope.notes: The matching identifier and U-M header support identity of the encoded item, but the exact download event and any repository-side byte transformations are undocumented.
ME Compendium added structural markup, line and folio references, and bracketed manuscript readings after obtaining the raw text from OTA.
The current OTA header says 'Transcribed from' the 1922 Gollancz edition.

object.description: Sole manuscript witness of Saint Erkenwald

scope.notes: Gollancz's preface gives ff. 72b-75a. Here a/b maps directly to r/v, but the endpoint 75a conflicts with Gollancz's own [F. 75b] marker before line 341, CME's 75b milestone, and the current BL/DIMEV endpoint 75v. The locator retains the corrected work extent while documenting the printed source-layer error.
Gollancz identifies Horstmann's text as the previous edition but explicitly bases his own text on a fresh manuscript transcript.

scope.notes: If the published object is restored, associate Saint Erkenwald with fols. 72v-75v and inventory the surrounding images before assigning whole-codex coverage. Folio 75v is shared with the following Life of St John the Baptist.

assertion.notes: The catalogue links this identifier to Harley MS 2250, but reports the images unavailable. The relationship to the manuscript is supported; the image coverage is unknown.

access.access_method: Purchase the Liverpool University Press edition or consult it through JSTOR or a holding library. No authorized complete public download was verified.
access.format: Printed scholarly edition and JSTOR digital edition

access.access_method: Open the exact BL Archives and Manuscripts record.
Use the BL collection-item request form with shelfmark Harley MS 2250. The manuscript must be consulted in London; the record requires a letter of introduction and Reader Pass conditions apply.
The exact BL record links a legacy digital object but states 'digital images currently unavailable.' Recheck the object; do not assume that the inaccessible sequence covers the complete codex.
access.format: Legacy digital page images; published extent unknown
notes: No manuscript facsimile was cached because no complete public image sequence could be accessed.
If the object returns, inventory every published image before assigning coverage. Saint Erkenwald occupies fols. 72v-75v, with f. 75v shared by the following Life of St John the Baptist.

access.access_method: Submit the British Library Imaging Services online order for colour photography of the complete physical Harley MS 2250, not only the poem leaves. Quote the shelfmark and request bindings, covers, pastedowns, flyleaves, every recto and verso, inserted leaves, unfoliated matter, blanks, and stubs where reproducible; map Saint Erkenwald separately to fols. 72v-75v.

local_copies.label: British Library digitisation and reproduction services price list (2025/26 rates)

access.contact: licensing@bl.uk for reproduction/licensing information; permissions@bl.uk for written permission under the Imaging Services terms
access.format: Colour photography of the original manuscript; ordinary scans are available only from surrogates; standard delivery is electronic
notes: The 2025/26 rate card lists photographic TIFF at £27.70 for the first image and £7.25 for each subsequent same-item image, or £58.19 per bespoke image; a complete-codex order requires an item-specific quotation.
The service says recto may be abbreviated r or a and verso v or b, confirming that CME's 72b-75b milestones map to 72v-75v rather than to a different foliation.
The requested full object includes substantially more than the target poem. Folio 75v is a shared boundary because the following Life of St John the Baptist begins on the same leaf.

Standard processing is stated as 30 working days after payment, but large or difficult orders can require different timing, handling, and prices. Supply of files does not itself authorize repository redistribution or publication.

rights.component: Underlying medieval Middle English poem and manuscript textual content
rights.basis: The poem and manuscript were created centuries before modern copyright. This does not govern modern photographs, editions, transcriptions, interfaces or provider terms.

rights.component: Gollancz's 1922 edition, transcription and editorial matter
rights.basis: The edition was published in 1922, before the current United States public-domain cutoff for published works.

rights.component: Horstmann's 1881 edition, transcription and editorial matter
rights.basis: The edition was published in 1881, before the current United States public-domain cutoff for published works.

rights.component: Turville-Petre's 2024 critical text, translation, introduction, annotations, glossary, and other editorial matter
rights.jurisdiction: Applicable copyright law and provider terms
rights.basis: This is a modern scholarly edition. No open license for the complete edition was found on the verified JSTOR, publisher/distributor, DIMEV, or review records.
notes: The public-domain status of the medieval poem does not place the edition's modern editorial expression or translation in the public domain.

editorial_practices.representation: Fresh transcript with editorial emendations marked by square brackets or obeli and deviations documented in notes
editorial_practices.description: Gollancz explicitly bases his text on a fresh transcript rather than copying Horstmann. His printed quatrain arrangement is editorial, and the edition requires its notes to distinguish manuscript readings from editorial intervention.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Manuscript contractions and final ornamental curls
editorial_practices.representation: Editorially expanded text; Gollancz rejects the earlier expansion of a final curl as -ne
editorial_practices.description: Gollancz's preface documents that contraction expansion depends on editorial judgement. The print edition, not the downstream plain text alone, must be consulted to understand those decisions; manuscript images are required to recover the original signs directly.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Gollancz's printed uppercase and lowercase distinctions
editorial_practices.description: OTA deliberately reduced the text to lowercase, so capitalization in the manuscript or print edition cannot be reconstructed from the OTA text.
OTA removed all punctuation; downstream punctuation-free text therefore does not represent either the manuscript directly or Gollancz's printed punctuation.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Gollancz's poem, textual notes and editorial apparatus
editorial_practices.representation: Poem text only; editorial notes omitted and Gollancz's emendations left unmarked
editorial_practices.description: The OTA header says that only the text was transcribed, not the editorial manuscript notes, and that Gollancz's emendations appear without indication. It also records 'th' for thorn, '3' for yogh and loss of some editorial hyphenation.

editorial_practices.source_feature: OTA plain text compared to Gollancz's print edition
editorial_practices.representation: Modern-English opener and argument, structural navigation, line numbers and folio milestones added; Gollancz's emended reading retained in the line with the manuscript reading added in square-bracketed '[MS: ...]' form
editorial_practices.description: ME Compendium compared the OTA file to Gollancz and restored explicit evidence of manuscript variants for editorial emendations. For example, line 1 reads Gollancz's 'tyme' followed by '[MS: sythen]'. Its [PROLOGUE] at lines 1-33 and Fit I/Fit II boundaries at lines 34 and 177 are encoding-level navigation, not a transcription of a three-part manuscript structure: checked scholarship reports manuscript capitals only at lines 1 and 177 and no further manuscript or strophic divisions. This layer is still mediated through Gollancz rather than a new direct manuscript collation.
editorial_practices.scope: Complete CME90053 body, including modern editorial framing and the 352-line encoded poem

evidence.citation: Structural and fixity audit of CME90053.xml using repository inspection tooling.
evidence.locator: Complete file; inspect-corpus-source.py CME90053
evidence.summary: The audit resolved the approved explicit override, confirmed 28,250 bytes, SHA-256 and git blob, and counted one poem, three internal DIV2 divisions, four heads, 352 L elements, seven folio milestones, one note, one BODY argument P, and three HEADER P elements.

evidence.citation: Israel Gollancz, ed., St. Erkenwald, Select Early English Poems 4 (London: Humphrey Milford, Oxford University Press, 1922), Internet Archive sterkenwaldbisho00erkeuoft.
evidence.locator: Title page; preface pp. v-viii and lv-lxii; poem printed pp. 1-14 / PDF pp. 73-86
evidence.summary: The complete edition confirms its title and publication, sole witness, fresh-transcript claim, consultation of Horstmann, editorial quatrains, emendation signs, contraction-expansion policy, conjectural 1386 dating, speculative Ralph Strode attribution and the complete 352-line text. Its preface gives ff. 72b-75a, but the poem marks [F. 75b] before line 341.
notes: Cached complete edition: source-cache/CME90053/gollancz-1922-st-erkenwald-complete-edition.pdf.

evidence.citation: Carl Horstmann, Altenglische Legenden, Neue Folge (Heilbronn: Gebr. Henninger, 1881), 265-274, Internet Archive altenglischeleg00barbgoog.
evidence.locator: Printed pages 265-274 / PDF pages 468-479
evidence.summary: The complete volume confirms Horstmann's predecessor edition, its manuscript source and the complete work text; Gollancz's preface establishes that he consulted this edition while preparing a fresh transcript.
notes: Cached complete volume: source-cache/CME90053/horstmann-1881-altenglische-legenden-neue-folge-complete.pdf.

evidence.citation: DIMEV record 706, 'At London in England not full long sithen.'
evidence.locator: v1.0 data/Records.xml, record-706
evidence.summary: Record 706 identifies the poem as a Life of Saint Erkenwald in alliterative unrhymed verse, gives its incipit and explicit, records one witness at Harley MS 2250 fols. 72v-75v, supplies the manuscript title and lists editions including Horstmann, Gollancz and Turville-Petre 2024.

evidence.citation: DIMEV v1.0 manuscript entry BLHar2250.
evidence.locator: data/Manuscripts.xml, item BLHar2250
evidence.summary: The manuscript entry assigns fols. 72v-75v to eLALME Linguistic Profile 419, grid 382 369, Cheshire, distinguishing that work-specific dialect evidence from other hands and sections of Harley MS 2250.

evidence.citation: Stefan Schustereder, 'Coming to Terms with a Pagan Past: The Story of St Erkenwald,' Studia Anglica Posnaniensia 48.2-3 (2013), 71-92, doi:10.2478/stap-2013-0008.
evidence.locator: pp. 72-76 and notes 2-16
evidence.summary: The peer-reviewed study reviews the unique witness and 1477 scribal connection; reports Peterson's probably-post-1386 and no-later-than-1420 dating; identifies John Massey of Cotton as Peterson's authorship candidate without consensus; reports manuscript capitals only at lines 1 and 177 and no further manuscript or strophic divisions; distinguishes the first 32-line historical introduction from later editorial structure; and reviews unresolved Pearl/Gawain common-authorship claims.
notes: The ignored local cache is the complete 22-page article and is 214,782 bytes.

evidence.citation: Thorlac Turville-Petre, ed., St Erkenwald: A Critical Edition, Exeter Medieval Texts and Studies (Liverpool: Liverpool University Press, 2024).
evidence.locator: JSTOR book record; DIMEV Bibliography.xml key Turville-Petre2024; publisher/distributor record
evidence.summary: Confirms the current critical edition's editor, title, publisher and 2024 publication. DIMEV cites it, and the independent Medieval Review account describes its facing-page Middle English text and translation, introduction, textual notes, annotations, manuscript-gloss appendix and reference matter.
notes: The full edition was not publicly downloadable and was not cached. No unverified direct-transcription edge was added to the primary lineage.

evidence.citation: Dominique Battles, review of Thorlac Turville-Petre, St Erkenwald: A Critical Edition, The Medieval Review 25.09.30 (2025).
evidence.locator: Complete review, especially paragraphs describing contents, Cheshire context and authorship
evidence.summary: The review confirms the edition's contents and reports that Turville-Petre challenges the conventional 'London poem' label, argues for a Cheshire audience and patronal context, and reopens but does not resolve common authorship with the Pearl-poet corpus.

evidence.citation: Stephen Gordon, 'Conjuring the Dead in St Erkenwald,' The Review of English Studies 77.330 (2026): 286-299, doi:10.1093/res/hgaf097.
evidence.locator: Complete article; manuscript and composition context on p. 1 and analysis of the conjuration at lines 179-184 throughout
evidence.summary: Current peer-reviewed scholarship on the poem's conjuration scene. The article identifies Harley MS 2250 as the unique witness, cites Turville-Petre's 2023 Cheshire-miscellany study, and contextualizes lines 179-184 within medieval exorcism, discernment and apotropaic practice.
notes: The ignored local cache is the complete 611,643-byte accepted PDF. The article is published open access under CC BY 4.0.

evidence.citation: U.S. Copyright Office, Circular 15A, Duration of Copyright.
evidence.locator: Term chart for works first published before the current statutory cutoff
evidence.summary: The Copyright Office term chart supports the public-domain status in the United States of the 1881 and 1922 editions as of 2026.

evidence.citation: General Corpus README, rights and licensing statement.
evidence.locator: License statement
evidence.summary: The repository distinguishes code licensing and project output licensing. Source-specific embedded licenses still need to be modeled and followed independently.

open_questions.question: Can restored manuscript images confirm the exact placement of every internal CME folio milestone against the current BL pencil foliation?
next_steps: Treat a/b and r/v as equivalent side notation: 72b=72v and 75b=75v.
Preserve Gollancz/OTA's 75a endpoint as a documented source-layer error because Gollancz's poem, CME, BL and DIMEV support 75b/75v.
When images return or are supplied, verify the line-to-folio transitions at CME milestones 72b, 73a, 73b, 74a, 74b, 75a and 75b without reopening the already established a/b crosswalk.

open_questions.question: Can the poem's composition date, place or author be narrowed beyond a late-fourteenth- or early-fifteenth-century anonymous Northwest Midland work associated with London?
next_steps: Treat Gollancz's 1386 date and London-author argument as attributed conjectures rather than facts.
Consult the complete Turville-Petre 2024 edition, beyond its verified bibliographic record and independent review, before adopting its Cheshire audience, patron or author profile as fact.
Keep the Gawain/Pearl Poet, Ralph Strode and John Massey of Cotton claims as low-confidence attribution history unless stronger evidence or consensus emerges.
Keep witness dialect, literary setting and place of composition as separate assertions.

open_questions.question: Does the inaccessible legacy BL object contain a complete Harley MS 2250 sequence, and what item-specific supply and reuse terms would govern a commissioned complete-codex facsimile?
next_steps: Recheck the exact LARK object and ask Imaging Services whether a complete preservation image set already exists; do not infer whole-codex coverage from the identifier alone.

If ordering images, request the whole physical manuscript, including bindings, endleaves, blanks, inserts and both sides of every leaf, and map Saint Erkenwald separately to fols. 72v-75v with the shared f. 75v boundary recorded.

Use the current order form and obtain written terms from licensing@bl.uk and permissions@bl.uk for Git redistribution and commercial or noncommercial print-on-demand use before publishing supplied images.

open_questions.question: How should future commercially usable corpus outputs separate public-domain source text from the CC BY-NC-SA 3.0 OTA/U-M encoding and the repository's broader output-license statement?
next_steps: Do not assume that public-domain status of the medieval poem or Gollancz edition removes the noncommercial condition from copied OTA/U-M expression.
Seek permission or prepare a demonstrably independent transcription from a public-domain edition or manuscript facsimile for commercial distribution.
Document which textual layer each generated artifact actually uses.

open_questions.question: What exact U-M release, download event and repository-side transformation produced the pinned CME90053 XML blob?
next_steps: Inspect CME repository history and surviving U-M export documentation.
Add a copied_from relation only if an exact byte-level transfer can be documented.

review_notes: The primary transmission path is artifact -> U-M CME encoding -> OTA 0053 -> Gollancz 1922 -> Harley MS 2250; Horstmann 1881 is a consulted predecessor, not the immediate source of OTA or U-M.
The unique medieval witness is kept distinct from the abstract poem, printed editions, digital encodings, catalog descriptions and scans.

Legacy a/b and current r/v are equivalent leaf-side notation. The only substantive locator conflict is Gollancz/OTA's 75a endpoint, preserved as a source-layer error against Gollancz's own 75b milestone and the CME/BL/DIMEV endpoint 75b/75v.

Verified public supporting downloads include complete Gollancz and Horstmann scans, the complete DIMEV release, OTA files, Schustereder's article, Gordon's CC BY 4.0 article and the BL rate card. No manuscript images were cached because the exact legacy BL object currently reports that its images are unavailable.
